abstract: Multicomponent reactions (MCRs) represent a cornerstone of modern sustainable chemistry, offering high atom economy and procedural efficiency through the one-pot synthesis of complex molecular architectures. Despite their long history and widespread application in diversity-oriented synthesis, the precise mechanistic pathways of many fundamental MCRs remain subjects of investigation and academic debate. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of the current mechanistic understanding of five quintessential MCRs: the Biginelli, Hantzsch, Mannich, Passerini, and Ugi reactions. We critically evaluate the experimental and theoretical evidence underpinning various proposed reaction pathways, including the roles of iminium, enamine, and Knoevenagel intermediates. By synthesizing findings from NMR spectroscopy, mass spectrometry, and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, this manuscript highlights how reaction conditions, catalysts, and solvent effects dictate mechanistic preferences. Additionally, we address persistent controversies and side-reaction competitions, offering insights into the rational design of more efficient, selective, and environmentally benign catalytic systems for the production of biologically active compounds and industrial intermediates.
